title: typeof os.networkInterfaces family is a number (v18.0.0)
body:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v18.x/docs/api/os.html#osnetworkinterfaces

### Description of the problem

according to this commit: https://github.com/nodejs/node/commit/3a26db9697a09915a0d541d97f047fc9c02bb452 , the type of os.networkInterfaces family is an integer now, but the api doc still show as string. 

Another problem about this change, some functions of package **require('ip')** may not working. error message: details.family.toLowerCase is not a function

detail code: https://github.com/indutny/node-ip/blob/master/lib/ip.js#L385

(Just bring it up incase someone get the same error

title: Update V8 after they switch to final Wasm GC opcodes and turn it on by default (~ 12 Sep 2023)
body:
### What is the problem this feature will solve?

Wasm GC is close to moving to the final Phase 4 (~Sep 12).
Before that, there is [the plan to clean up opcodes](https://github.com/WebAssembly/gc/pull/372), which will be a breaking change. And it would be nice to have a Node.js version compatible with new final opcodes as soon as possible.

(We are using Node.js to run wasm binaries, produced by Kotlin/Wasm toolchain, outside browsers.)

### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

Updating V8

### What alternatives have you considered?

Using other runtimes :)
text:

(We are using Node.js to run wasm binaries, produced by Kotlin/Wasm toolchain, outside browsers.)

### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

Updating V8

### What alternatives have you considered?

Using other runtimes :)

title: IncomingMessage docs incorrect
body:
https://github.com/nodejs/undici/issues/1115

We have missed updating the docs.

> This property is guaranteed to be an instance of the <net.Socket> class, a subclass of <stream.Duplex>, unless the user specified a socket type other than <net.Socket>.

Is no longer true. It will be null after detach and destroy.

https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/265a47d680077945e49578f4cb0decc772ff8058/lib/_http_server.js#L246-L250
https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/265a47d680077945e49578f4cb0decc772ff8058/lib/_http_client.js#L661-L666
https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/265a47d680077945e49578f4cb0decc772ff8058/lib/internal/streams/destroy.js#L339-L341

Title: IncomingMessage docs incorrect

Body:
https://github.com/nodejs/undici/issues/1115

We have missed updating the docs.

> This property is guaranteed to be an instance of the <net.Socket> class, a subclass of <stream.Duplex>, unless the user specified a socket type other than <net.Socket>.

Is no longer true. It will be null after detach and destroy.

https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/265a47d680077945e49578f4cb0decc772ff8058/lib/_http_server.js#L246-L250
https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/265a47d680077945e49578f4cb0decc772ff8058/lib/_http_client.js#L661-L666
https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/265a47d680077945e49578f4cb0decc772ff8058/lib/internal/streams/destroy.js#L339-L341

title: `atob` should throw an error if the input is not correctly encoded
body:
### Version

17.9.0

### Platform

MacOS 12.2

### Subsystem

global / buffer

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

_No response_

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

_No response_

### What is the expected behavior?

```js
atob('a') // => DOMException
atob('a ') // => DOMException
atob('aaaaa') // => DOMException
```

### What do you see instead?

```js
atob('a') === ''
atob('a ') === ''
atob('aaaaa') === 'i¦\x9A'
```

### Additional information

https://infra.spec.whatwg.org/#forgiving-base64-decode step 3

Related to #42539 / #42530 @aduh95

title: Browsing through node.js docs should be made easy
body:
### What is the problem this feature will solve?

Going through the docs is already a hectic task. It can be made easy with some features.

### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

Search bar in the docs to go directly to the function, or related keyword.
And the site map should be present on the node.js site, especially for docs if possible also there should be a search bar on that site map.

### What alternatives have you considered?

Google search directly to take me to the function directly or the chatGpt to give me the function like that directly but it becomes hard when you don't know the basics.

And the site map should be present on the node.js site, especially for docs if possible also there should be a search bar on that site map.

### What alternatives have you considered?

Google search directly to take me to the function directly or the chatGpt to give me the function like that directly but it becomes hard when you don't know the basics.

title: test-runner: global `after` not working
body:
### Version

20.2.0

### Platform

Linux 6.3.3-arch1-1 x86_64 GNU/Linux

### Subsystem

_No response_

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

`node --test test.js`

```js
const { before, after, test } = require('node:test')

before(() => console.log('before'))
after(() => console.log('after'))

test('test', () => console.log('test'))
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

always

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

Expect printing `after` on stdout

### What do you see instead?

Only see `before` and `test` printing on stdout

### Additional information

_No response_

title: Fastest way to pass data from child process to worker thread?
body:
What is the faster way to pass e.g. stderr from a child process to a worker thread (for processing) without putting load on the main thread?

I've tried a two different things without success:

- Pass `worker.stdin` to `child_process.spawn stdio: ['pipe', 'pipe', worker.stdin]`
- Try to pass child_process.stderr file descriptor to worker thread

What is the recommended way of doing this? Is it possible to efficiently pipe data from a child process to a worker thread?

I'm using workers to reduce load on the main thread for parsing the output. However, transferring the data also puts a lot of load on the main thread.
text:

Body:

- Try to pass child_process.stderr file descriptor to worker thread

What is the recommended way of doing this? Is it possible to efficiently pipe data from a child process to a worker thread?

I'm using workers to reduce load on the main thread for parsing the output. However, transferring the data also puts a lot of load on the main thread.

title: import {getColorDepth} from 'tty'
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
The `getColorDepth` and `hasColors` functions are currently attached to the `tty.WriteStream` prototype, even though they seem fairly standalone (their result [depends on the env](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/ae0cfc95d55f35b76be7341969d6ad36dcc000b7/lib/internal/tty.js#L103-L213), not the stream). As a result, accessing them "legally" (without extracting them from the prototype) isn't possible when `process.stdout` & co aren't proper ttys.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
It would be useful to be able to just import `getColorDepth` & friends from `tty`.
text:
Title: import {getColorDepth} from 'tty'

Body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
The `getColorDepth` and `hasColors` functions are currently attached to the `tty.WriteStream` prototype, even though they seem fairly standalone (their result [depends on the env](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/ae0cfc95d55f35b76be7341969d6ad36dcc000b7/lib/internal/tty.js#L103-L213), not the stream). As a result, accessing them "legally" (without extracting them from the prototype) isn't possible when `process.stdout` & co aren't proper ttys.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
It would be useful to be able to just import `getColorDepth` & friends from `tty`.

title: Can it be judged that the cursor is at the beginning of a line
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/orgs/nodejs/discussions/48046
Originally posted by **btea** May 17, 2023 Sometimes when I want to use `console.log` to print messages to the terminal, if I use `process.stdout.write` to output content on the terminal elsewhere, the content printed by console.log will be spliced ​​at the end, which does not look very friendly . At this point, I want to judge whether the current cursor is at the beginning of a line, if so, print the information directly, if not, print `\n` first, and then print the information.
text:

Originally posted by **btea** May 17, 2023 Sometimes when I want to use `console.log` to print messages to the terminal, if I use `process.stdout.write` to output content on the terminal elsewhere, the content printed by console.log will be spliced ​​at the end, which does not look very friendly . At this point, I want to judge whether the current cursor is at the beginning of a line, if so, print the information directly, if not, print `\n` first, and then print the information.

title: Feature Request: export ExternOneByteString for addons
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

With MSVC on Windows, node addons cannot link properly if they subclass `v8::String::ExternalOneByteStringResource`, forcing devs to disable the win_delay_load_hook which affects projects like Electron.

Supposedly disabling (some?) MSVC optimizations on Windows should work around the issue, but I was not successful when I tried it.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Is it technically possible for node to export its own subclasses (at least `ExternOneByteString` from src/string_bytes.cc) and addons could use/link that successfully?
text:
Title: Feature Request: export ExternOneByteString for addons

Body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

With MSVC on Windows, node addons cannot link properly if they subclass `v8::String::ExternalOneByteStringResource`, forcing devs to disable the win_delay_load_hook which affects projects like Electron.

Supposedly disabling (some?) MSVC optimizations on Windows should work around the issue, but I was not successful when I tried it.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Is it technically possible for node to export its own subclasses (at least `ExternOneByteString` from src/string_bytes.cc) and addons could use/link that successfully?

title: crypto.sign swallows OpenSSL errors
body:
### Version

current

### Platform

all

### Subsystem

crypto

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

```js
crypto.sign('sha512', '', crypto.generateKeyPairSync('rsa', { modulusLength: 512 }).privateKey)
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

One of the internal OpenSSL calls must fail, in this case, due to incompatible digest and modulus length.

### What is the expected behavior?

A useful error message.

### What do you see instead?

```
Uncaught Error: Deriving bits failed
    at Object.signOneShot [as sign] (node:internal/crypto/sig:181:42)
```

### Additional information

_No response_

title: Add depth/breadth first search algorithms to recursive readdir
body:
### What is the problem this feature will solve?

Continuation of #34992 and #41439

This feature will add specific algorithm support to the upcoming recursive readdir feature I'm working on in #41439 

### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

The api should be extended to include a new parameter to the `options` object with the type:

```ts
{
  algorithm?: 'depth-first' | 'breadth-first'
}
```

I've already stubbed out tests for this feature based on the early work in #41439 see this fork-branch for more details:
https://github.com/Ethan-Arrowood/node/tree/feature/recursive-readdir-dfs-bfs
text:

Continuation of #34992 and #41439

This feature will add specific algorithm support to the upcoming recursive readdir feature I'm working on in #41439

title: There is no description of function overloading in the documentation `net.createServer`
body:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v18.x/docs/api/net.html#netcreateserveroptions-connectionlistener

### Description of the problem


**Function overload description missing**   --> [net.createServer([options][, connectionListener])](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v18.x/docs/api/net.html#netcreateserveroptions-connectionlistener) !

**Add a description of function overloading to the documentation!**

``function createServer(connectionListener?: (socket: Socket) => void): Server;``
``function createServer(options?: ServerOpts, connectionListener?: (socket: Socket) => void): Server;``
text:
Title: There is no description of function overloading in the documentation `net.createServer`

Body:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v18.x/docs/api/net.html#netcreateserveroptions-connectionlistener

### Description of the problem


**Function overload description missing**   --> [net.createServer([options][, connectionListener])](https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v18.x/docs/api/net.html#netcreateserveroptions-connectionlistener) !

**Add a description of function overloading to the documentation!**

``function createServer(connectionListener?: (socket: Socket) => void): Server;``
``function createServer(options?: ServerOpts, connectionListener?: (socket: Socket) => void): Server;``

title: doc: note readable/writeable .toWeb()/.fromWeb() exist in webstreams docs
body:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/api/webstreams.html

### Description of the problem

currently, there's no indication on the webstreams docs that `.toWeb()` and `.fromWeb()` exist. Noting that they exist somewhere on the webstreams docs would _probably_ be a good idea. I just watched two very capable engineers spend longer than they should have figure out that these features existed while trying to use `fetch` and streams because they _weren't_ in the webstreams docs - if they're tripping over this, I very much imagine it will be something that comes up again for others.

title: [node:util] `format` is optional in `util.format`?
body:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v18.x/api/util.html#utilformatformat-args

### Description of the problem

By https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v18.x/api/util.html#utilformatformat-args has required string argument `util.format`.

But in examples we see:
> If the first argument does not contain a valid format specifier, `util.format()` returns a string that is the concatenation of all arguments separated by spaces:
> ```js
> util.format(1, 2, 3);
> // Returns: '1 2 3'
> ```

Looks like it the same as having *optional* `format` argument.
So it shoud be `util.format([format [, ...args]])` instead of `util.format(format[, ...args])`.
text:
Title: [node:util] `format` is optional in `util.format`?

Body:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v18.x/api/util.html#utilformatformat-args

### Description of the problem

By https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v18.x/api/util.html#utilformatformat-args has required string argument `util.format`.

But in examples we see:
> If the first argument does not contain a valid format specifier, `util.format()` returns a string that is the concatenation of all arguments separated by spaces:
> ```js
> util.format(1, 2, 3);
> // Returns: '1 2 3'
> ```

Looks like it the same as having *optional* `format` argument.
So it shoud be `util.format([format [, ...args]])` instead of `util.format(format[, ...args])`.

title: Does `Buffer.from` copy or reference?
body:
This is a little unclear from the documentation. Does `Buffer.from(arrayBuffer)` copy the argument or just create a new  buffer that references the argument.

From the documentation and the code as well as https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Uint8Array/Uint8Array I get the impression that it's a copy.

However, while running real-life code I do encounter detached buffer errors using this method and instead I'm forced to do something like:

```js
buf = Buffer.from(arrayBuffer) // Not enough.
buf = Buffer.from(Uint8Array.prototype.slice.call(buf)) // Force copy.
```

In order to ensure I don't get any detached buffers.
text:
Title: Does `Buffer.from` copy or reference?

Body:
This is a little unclear from the documentation. Does `Buffer.from(arrayBuffer)` copy the argument or just create a new  buffer that references the argument.

From the documentation and the code as well as https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Uint8Array/Uint8Array I get the impression that it's a copy.

However, while running real-life code I do encounter detached buffer errors using this method and instead I'm forced to do something like:

```js
buf = Buffer.from(arrayBuffer) // Not enough.
buf = Buffer.from(Uint8Array.prototype.slice.call(buf)) // Force copy.
```

In order to ensure I don't get any detached buffers.

title: timers: recent performance regressions
body:
0d9f3bd9e87d648c5f7d64fa402d01792bc795f5 introduced a noticeable performance regression with various timers benchmarks. For example:

* `timers/immediate.js type="clear"` performance dropped 77%
* `timers/timers-cancel-pooled.js` performance dropped 55%
* `timers/timers-timeout-pooled.js` performance dropped 41%

and many other timers benchmarks/benchmark configs saw double digit drops.

While having a public "get active requests"/"get active handles" API would be nice, I don't think it is worth this kind of penalty if we can't find some way to remedy the performance regressions.

/cc @RaisinTen
text:
Title: timers: recent performance regressions

Body:
0d9f3bd9e87d648c5f7d64fa402d01792bc795f5 introduced a noticeable performance regression with various timers benchmarks. For example:

* `timers/immediate.js type="clear"` performance dropped 77%
* `timers/timers-cancel-pooled.js` performance dropped 55%
* `timers/timers-timeout-pooled.js` performance dropped 41%

and many other timers benchmarks/benchmark configs saw double digit drops.

While having a public "get active requests"/"get active handles" API would be nice, I don't think it is worth this kind of penalty if we can't find some way to remedy the performance regressions.

/cc @RaisinTen

title: Duplex.from failure
Linked modules do not get added to the built-in modules
body:
### Version

v17.7.0

### Platform

Windows

### Subsystem

_No response_

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

Compile NodeJS and add a linked module by e.g. using this command:
```bash
vcbuild.bat x64 dll link-module ./my-extra-module.js
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

_No response_

### What is the expected behavior?

The module should be available as a built-in module when using the resulting binary.

### What do you see instead?

The file is missing from the file generated by the `js2c` script, presumably by faulty refactoring done in the past. (It still worked with NodeJS v14)

### Additional information

_No response_

title: `worker.postMessage` performance
body:
I'm using `worker.postMessage(myString)` to send data to my worker. However, I'm getting significant performance overhead from `postMessage`. I'm spending 16% of my total cpu time here. The strings are usually quite short but can be up to 256 chars.

Where does the overhead of `postMessage` mostly come from and is there ways to improve it? I might be intersted in trying to improve it if someone could point me in the right direction.

I've been consdering using a `SharedArrayBuffer` and writing the strings into the buffer and then `Atomics.wait/notify`, However, that feels wrong as it basically just re-implements what `postMessage` could/should be doing anyway. Is `MessageChannel` faster?
text:
Title: `worker.postMessage` performance

Body:
I'm using `worker.postMessage(myString)` to send data to my worker. However, I'm getting significant performance overhead from `postMessage`. I'm spending 16% of my total cpu time here. The strings are usually quite short but can be up to 256 chars.

Where does the overhead of `postMessage` mostly come from and is there ways to improve it? I might be intersted in trying to improve it if someone could point me in the right direction.

I've been consdering using a `SharedArrayBuffer` and writing the strings into the buffer and then `Atomics.wait/notify`, However, that feels wrong as it basically just re-implements what `postMessage` could/should be doing anyway. Is `MessageChannel` faster?

title: question about `connectToMainThread` API of `inspector` module
body:
### Version

none

### Platform

none

### Subsystem

none

### What steps will reproduce the bug?

_No response_

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

none

### What is the expected behavior?

none

### What do you see instead?

none

### Additional information

Can we use [connectToMainThread](connectToMainThread) API to send a `Runtime.evaluate` command to V8 inspector for running JavaScript code ? Because [connectToMainThread](connectToMainThread) is implemented by `RequestInterrupt`, and this [discussion](https://groups.google.com/g/v8-users/c/nuZ_aEK7_Mc) said that JavaScript code should not be called in `RequestInterrupt` callback.

### Additional information

Can we use [connectToMainThread](connectToMainThread) API to send a `Runtime.evaluate` command to V8 inspector for running JavaScript code ? Because [connectToMainThread](connectToMainThread) is implemented by `RequestInterrupt`, and this [discussion](https://groups.google.com/g/v8-users/c/nuZ_aEK7_Mc) said that JavaScript code should not be called in `RequestInterrupt` callback.
